title: A Journey into Covenant Theology - Part 2
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: A Journey into Covenant Theology - Part 2 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Historical Origin and Objections to the Covenant of Redemption
subpoints: The term first appears in 1638 at the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland (David Dixon)
Further developed by Goodwin, Fisher, Olin, and Durham over the following decade
O. Palmer Robertson's objection: no specific creedal development; covenant requires blood ratification
Robertson's definition of covenant as 'a bond in blood' does not encompass all biblical covenants (e.g., Noah, David, Gibeonites)

outline.title: The Doctrine Throughout Church History
subpoints: Arminius (1603) explicitly references a covenant between Father and Son based on Psalm 2:8
Theodore Beza (1567) translated Luke 22:29 using the word 'covenant' for the Father-Son relationship
Augustine and Chrysostom engaged the Father-Son economic relationship in the 4th-5th centuries
The title is new, but the doctrine runs through all of church history

outline.title: Scriptural Pillars of the Covenant of Redemption
subpoints: Dixon observed transactional (buying/selling) language throughout Scripture implies prior agreement
Christ regularly speaks of his earthly mission in terms of fulfilling what the Father gave him to do (John 17:4)
Ephesians 1:3-14 shows Father choosing (vv. 3-6), Son accomplishing (vv. 7-12), Spirit sealing (vv. 13-14)
Christ's work in the covenant of redemption constitutes a covenant of works for him on behalf of the elect

outline.title: The Role of Each Person of the Trinity
subpoints: The Father sends and chooses the people given to the Son
The Son accomplishes redemption through incarnation, obedience, and sacrificial death
The Spirit applies the work of Christ to the elect and is the guarantee of the inheritance
Each person has distinct, vital, and non-interchangeable roles in the covenant

outline.title: Why the Covenant of Redemption Matters
subpoints: It is the surety of the elect: God cannot break his covenantal oath, so salvation cannot be undone
It guards against speculation by pointing us to Christ as the visible revelation of the eternal decree
It communicates the incredible love of God — the triune God's love for his people and for one another
It gives the believer assurance and joy in knowing redemption rests solely on the work of Christ

summary:
In this second installment of his personal journey into covenant theology, Mr. Isaiah Couch examines the Covenant of Redemption — the eternal, intra-Trinitarian agreement between Father, Son, and Holy Spirit to accomplish the redemption of the elect. He begins by acknowledging his earlier skepticism, which he shared with theologians like O. Palmer Robertson, who questioned whether the doctrine rested on sufficient scriptural warrant and whether a covenant required blood ratification. Couch then demonstrates that Robertson's own definition is too narrow, failing to account for covenants with Noah, David, and the Gibeonites that involve no blood ceremony.

Moving through church history, Couch shows that while the title 'Covenant of Redemption' appears in 1638 with David Dixon, the doctrine itself is present in Arminius, Theodore Beza, Augustine, Chrysostom, and many others. The language and imagery of an eternal Father-Son agreement to redeem the elect permeates the writings of the church across centuries, even when the precise label is absent. The doctrine rests not on a single proof text but on pillar after pillar of scriptural evidence.

The heart of the lecture is an exposition of Ephesians 1:3-14, where Couch traces the distinct roles of each Trinitarian person: the Father choosing and predestinating (vv. 3-6), the Son accomplishing redemption through his blood (vv. 7-12), and the Spirit sealing and guaranteeing the inheritance of the elect (vv. 13-14). Dixon's observation that Scripture regularly uses transactional language — buying, selling, purchasing — to describe redemption presupposes a prior agreement between the parties, which is the Covenant of Redemption.

Couch closes with three practical applications: the covenant is the surety of the elect, since God cannot break his covenantal oath; it guards against speculation by directing us to Christ rather than into the hidden decrees; and it reveals the breathtaking love of the triune God, who before all time set his love upon his people and committed himself fully to their redemption.
transcript:
Good morning everyone. As Pastor T.2 said, I know Scott Hollm there has a thing about never apologize when you be good at lesson, but I do want to apologize about when I was making those requests over and over and over again. I wish I had more time, I wish I had more time. And so thank you to the elders and in allowing me to speak. I have the honor again to speak on you on my journey in the covenant theology and just a brief summary of what we discussed last time. I gave an overview of the three foundational cabinets in my journey in regards to covenant theology. We began with covenant of redemption and how it is the eternal, interchurned, tearing covenant between the father, the son, and the spirit in accomplishing the redemption of the elect. I shared a quote from Arcee Sproll and stood out to me in my own journey that summarizes how this covenant is accomplished. He says, it is the father who sends the son into the world to redeem his people. It is the son who accomplishes that redemption by his work of obedience and it is the spirit that applies the work of Christ to his people. It is the spirit who brings us to the son who reconciles us to the father. This is the covenant that we will be looking at closer this morning. We then examined the covenant of works. We saw how Adam being humanity's covenantal head violated the terms of the covenant that God entered into with him in the garden. The covenant of works brings only death under Adam's headship, but under the covenantal headship of Christ, the true and better Adam it brings life to those who are electing him. I hope to examine this covenant in the month of June. Then we briefly close by looking at the covenant of grace. We saw how this covenant began immediately after the fall and God's promise to Adam and Eve and that he would put enmity between the seed of the serpent and the seed of the woman. We also looked at a quote from Herman Bavring where he talks about how this plays up practically in the church. He says in his reformed dogmatics, the covenant of grace is about not only individual persons, but also organic holes, including families and generations. Therefore, some who remain inwardly unbelieving will for a time in the earthly administration and dispensation of the covenant of grace will be part of the covenant people. The final judgment belongs to God alone and in this life the church must regard such with judgment of charity. I hope to examine that one in the month of July. These three lessons. So brief summary of what we previously discussed. So I'd like to take a closer look at the covenant of redemption in my own journey this morning. So you have your outlines. I went to my outline goes looking at the historical origin, the development, an understanding of this covenant, an examining scripture and how it speaks about this topic and finally close with addressing why does this matter. I'll be interviewing my own journey into this as well.

## Historical Origin and Objections

The historical origin and objection to this covenant. Historically, the covenant of redemption seems to suddenly appear on the scene in the middle of the 1600s. In 1638, the first recorded use that we have is from David Dixon at the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland. He gave a lecture that was titled, Arminianism Discussed, where he corrects some of the areas of Arminianism or addresses some of the areas of Arminianism and he presents the Reformed View which contains the covenant of redemption. And over the course of the next decade from 1638 to 1648, Dixon and several other well-known theologians, Thomas Goodwin, who was a member of the Westminster Assembly, Edward Fisher, John Olin and James Durham would write several papers using the term covenant of redemption when referring to the eternal redemptive activity between the father and the spirit.

So early on in my own journey, when I begin to study about covenant theology and become familiar with these terms, this term for me, the title, the covenant of redemption, I thought at first it was very speculative and I had questions and reservations about it. Why does this term suddenly seem to appear out of nowhere in the middle of the 17th century? Shouldn't things that arise very quickly give us reason to pause in a way. We see that through Church of History. And these questions and reservations that I was experiencing were already shared by several other men within the Reformed community, men like Carl Barth, John Murray, Robert Latham, and the Palmer Robertson. And specifically, the Palmer Robertson, I want to focus on because I highly recommend his book, The Christ of the Covenants. But in his book, he does bring up some objections and reservations to this covenant that I shared with him. And so the first quote from Robert said there, he says, this particular covenant finds no specific development in the classic creeds of the Reformeds of the 16th and 17th century. But it has been recognized broadly among covenant theologians since that time. A sense of artificiality flavors the effort to structure in covenantal terms, the mysteries of God's eternal councils.

So once again, at the time, I agreed with Robert's and being completely unfamiliar with these terms and understanding it, the sense of artificiality of speculation and even at the time of what seemed to be the forcing of covenantal terms into scripture. And this is also another point the Robertson makes. He says scripture simply does not say much of the pre-creation shape of the decrees of God. To speak concretely of an intertwinitarian covenant with terms and conditions between father and son is to extend the bounds of scripture evidence beyond propriety. And so Robertson concludes that those who hold to the understanding of a pre-creation covenant between the father and the son and by inclusion the spirit are assuming that a covenant is defined simply as a mutual contract or agreement. And not as he sees a covenant to be properly defined as, quote, a bond in blood or a bond in life and death sovereignly administered.

From Robertson's definition of covenant, it is required to be instituted by the shedding of blood. And he sees Hebrews 9:16-17 as giving the proper definition of a covenant. And so Hebrews 9:16-17 says, for where there is a testament, there must also be a necessity the death of the testator. For a testament is enforced after men are dead since it has no power at all while a testator lives. And this is where he calls this objection from. Therefore, not even the first covenant was dedicated without blood. When God makes a covenant with Abram in Genesis 15, we see the Suzerain-Vassal ceremony. We talked about this previously, which included the cutting or dividing of animals and laying opposite of one another and the shedding of blood. Genesis 15:10, then he brought all these to him and cut them into down the middle and placed each piece opposite the other and on that day the Lord made a covenant with Abram. Likewise, when God ratifies the covenant with the people of Israel Exodus 24:8, Moses took the blood sprinkled it on the people and said, this is the blood of the covenant, which the Lord has made with you. And we see it with Christ when he speaks of the new covenant, by saying, this cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is shed for you.

These examples meet the biblical requirement as upon the Robertson season to be defined as. Therefore, when he views this, what the covenant theologians would call the covenant of redemption, he says that, this does not meet the requirement as given by Scripture between the Father, the Son, and the Spirit. But as I continue to examine different writers, I begin to have some questions about Robertson's definition. I begin to see that his definition of covenant did not sufficiently encompass all covenant in Scripture. For example, God's covenant with Noah and with David, which Robertson agrees in his book The Christ of the Covenant are covenants. These are not instituted by blood. The covenant between David and Jonathan, 1 Samuel 18, where Jonathan made a covenant with David because he loved him as his own soul involves no shedding of blood and the dividing of animals. And the covenant between Israel and the Gibeonites in Joshua 9, was also not a covenant instituted by blood. And so these covenants are not only between men, between nations, and also between God and man. They do not always require a suzerain-vassal type ceremony, which included the cutting, dividing of animals in the shedding of blood.

## The Doctrine Throughout Church History

So with that objection and answers addressed, I began to examine two other primary objections that I shared with Robertson with regards to the covenant of redemption. One, its appearance onto the scene in the 17th century and two, its lack of development in the classic creeds of the reformers of the 16th and 17th century. Like I said, I must agree to the covenant that title, the covenant of redemption, has not been found in any writings as of yet before the 17th century. But I believe all of us must agree that many titles and terms seem to suddenly appear during the Reformation. The five solas, for example, Luther was proclaiming the doctrine of sola fide, before the term sola fide came to be. Augustine, which was more than a thousand years before the Reformation, was declaring the doctrine of sola gratia or grace alone and through faith alone long before the term was used.

I began to study whether the teachings and writings of theologians with regard to this eternal activity between the Father, Son, and Spirit was foreign to Church history before the 17th century. And the short answer being, it was not, it was not foreign at all. While Dixon's lecture in 1638 titled, Arminianism Discussed was addressing several errors of Arminianism at the time. Jacob Arminius himself in 1603 explicitly references the covenant between the Father and the Son. Now I know that quoting Jacob Arminius in a positive light might get me in trouble here. But I want to show that Arminius himself while he gets many things wrong, he does view the relationship between the Father and the Son to be a covenant. Arminius said, there in the outline, not only on the account of the decree of God is faith in Christ necessary, but it is also necessary on account of the promise made unto Christ by the Father and according to the covenant which was ratified between both of them. This is the word of that promise. Ask of me and I will give thee the heathen for thine inheritance, Psalm 2:8. Christ therefore by the decree, the promise, and the covenant of the Father, has been constituted the Savior of all that believe on Him.

Moving from the 17th century to the 16th century, I found Theodore Beza who was a student of John Calvin in 1567 while examining Jerome's translation of the Latin Vulgate was studying a specific passage of Luke 22:29 which reads in the Vulgate and I appoint unto you a kingdom as my Father appointed unto me a kingdom. Beza noted that Jerome used the term appoint to translate the Greek word for covenant and believed this to be an incorrect translation. So in Beza's translation of the New Testament he translated it as either for covenant to you just as my Father covenanted to me a kingdom. To borrow a quote from A.J.W. Fesco when speaking about Beza's translation here he says, Beza dropped an exegetical pebble in the theological pond and it rippled into the 17th century and I would even say that it has rippled through to our century today.

But even before the 16th century we can look all the way back to the late 4th or the 5th century with Augustine and Chrysostom. These two men engaged in discussion about the ontological or substance equality of the Father and the Son and the economic aspect of the Son's person and work and submission to the Father to accomplish the redemption of the elect. Augustine might not use the term the covenant of redemption but the language is there. The imagery is there. To borrow another example from Fesco, imagine you are in a room with one other person and all of a sudden that person gets a call. You have never met this person before but they get a call on their phone so they answer and so the conversation you only hear one side of it. And so imagine you are hearing this. They pick up. Hello. Hey Brian, yeah everything is here it is beautiful. Yep the pastor is here the bridegroom just finished the pictures the bride is ready the caterer is ready to go and the grandmother of the bride just arrived. Yes they picked up the first dance song this morning. Do you know if they picked out the last dance song? Great I'll let the DJ know. Yep I got the rings. Awesome. No one sitting in that room listening to one side of the conversation is going to say that was not a wedding being discussed because they did not use the word wedding. The scripture speaks the same way of this. Just because these different theologians throughout church history have not used the title the covenant of redemption, the language is there, the imagery is there.

## Scriptural Pillars of the Covenant of Redemption

The scripture speaks the same way. The doctrine of this covenant does not rest upon one specific proof text but it rests upon pillar after pillar after pillar, and if you remove one pillar it still stands secure and unmoved because the theologians throughout church history who have embraced this doctrine did so for one foundational reason and that is they believe that scripture taught it. The language of scripture pointed to it. The dialogue between the father and the son pointed to it and the teaching of individual passages proved it to be true.

Dixon observed that scripture clearly and regularly speaks of the redemption of the elect in transactional terms. Acts 20:28 — to shepherd the church of God which he purchased with his own blood. 1 Corinthians 6:20 — for you were bought at a price. Ephesians 1:7 — in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins according to the riches of his grace. 1 Peter 1:18-19 — you were not redeemed with corruptible things like silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ, who in verse 20 was foreordained before the foundation of the world.

Dixon pointed out that buying and selling presupposes that the parties involved have reached prior agreement regarding the terms and conditions of the transaction. Jesus himself regularly speaks of transactional or covenantal language with the father prior to his incarnation. Specifically John 17:4 — I have glorified you on the earth. I have finished the work which you have given me to do and now oh Father glorify me together with yourself with the glory which I had with you before the world was.

In understanding that the son agreed to the requirements given to him by the father, the covenant of redemption is to Christ a covenant of works. In order for the elect to be redeemed and saved from the wrath of God, Christ must accomplish the work given to him by the father. This work involves entering into time itself, taking upon flesh, being born of a woman, being born under the law, and being the substitutionary death for those chosen in him by the father from before the foundation of the world. Galatians 4:4-5 — but when the fullness of the time had come God sent forth his son born of a woman born under the law to redeem those who were under the law that we might receive the adoption of sons.

The spirit as the third person of the Godhead is also active and vital in fulfilling this covenant. The spirit always operates with the son and the father. Scripture speaks of the spirit's work in the incarnation, the resurrection, the spirit being poured out at Pentecost and the application of the work of Christ to the elect. The father does not become incarnate and die upon the cross. The son does not raise himself from the dead. The spirit does not send the son from eternity. But each person of the Godhead is seen as operating with distinct roles. The father sending, the son accomplishing, and the spirit applying.

I would like us to turn to Ephesians 1:3-14. This passage in my own journey shows the activity of each of the persons of the Godhead accomplishing the redemptive work of the elect. Ephesians 1:3-14:

*Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ. Just as he chose us in him before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and without blame before him in love, having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to himself, according to the good pleasure of his will, to the praise of the glory of his grace, by which he made us accepted in the beloved. In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins according to the riches of his grace, which he made to abound toward us in all wisdom and prudence, having made known to us the mystery of his will, according to his good pleasure which he purposed in himself, that in the dispensation of the fullness of the times he might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven and which are on earth in him. In him also we have obtained an inheritance being predestined according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will, that we who first trusted in Christ should be to the praise of his glory. In him you also trusted after you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, in him also having believed you were sealed with the Holy Spirit of promise, who is the guarantee of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession to the praise of his glory.*

In this passage we see the Father choosing in verses 3 through 6, the Son accomplishing the work given to him by the Father in verses 7 through 12, and the Spirit applying or sealing the work of Christ to the elect in verses 13 and 14.

## Why the Covenant of Redemption Matters

So application — why is this important? How does the covenant of redemption have any significance in my day to day life? Let me present three things that I found significant in my own life when I came to understand this covenant.

One, the covenant of redemption is the surety of the elect. The unchangeable covenant of God is our surety. Our salvation is sure. Because just as it is impossible for God to lie, it is impossible for our salvation to be undone. Scripture testifies to this in Philippians 1:6 — being confident of this very thing that he who has begun a good work in you will complete it until the day of Jesus Christ. The elect are safe because the Father has given them to the Son and Christ says that this is the will of Him who sent me, that of all that He has given me I shall lose nothing but raise it up at the last day, John 6:39. The Son accomplishes everything that He said He would do. And the spirit, as we read in Ephesians 1:13, is the guarantee of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession.

Number two, the covenant of redemption guards against speculation. Rather than speculate about the eternal decrees of God before the foundation of the world, this covenant points us directly to the mediator of this covenant. Michael Horton in his book God of Promise says that God's predestination is hidden to us, but Christ is not. The unveiling of the mystery hidden in past ages, the person and work of Christ becomes the only reliable testimony to our election. Those who trust in Christ, belong to Christ, are elect in Christ.

And three, the covenant of redemption communicates the incredible love of God. We have been chosen by the Father to be redeemed by the Son and that redemptive work applied to us by the Spirit. We should rejoice like John when he says in 1 John 3:1, behold what manner of love the Father has bestowed on us that we should be called children of God. The beautiful truth that God set his covenantal love upon his people and accomplished their redemption communicates not only the incredible love that God has for the elect but also communicates the incredible love that the persons of the Godhead have for one another.

Any questions? [Question and answer section regarding whether calling it a covenant rather than an agreement matters.] I think the importance of using the term covenant is because there are terms to a covenant, and a covenant is a sworn oath by God — and God does not break his covenant promises. So I now see that using covenant in terms I think is very important. I am not really answering your question. I am being a politician. I am still very new to covenant theology. This is still brand new to me. I am only two years into this.

Let us pray. Our Almighty God of covenant we thank you that before the foundation of the world you sovereignly chose to redeem a people for yourself. Through you sending the son, through the son accomplishing the work given to him, and through the spirit applying that work, we have been redeemed. And so we thank you for choosing not based on our own meritorious work but upon the work of the perfect mediator, the covenant of redemption, that being Christ. And I pray Father that as we think about this covenant that it would be of comfort to us to know that you have chosen, you have said, you have promised, and you will accomplish what you have sworn to do. We know that it is by grace that we have been saved. It is nothing we have done. So we thank you for this covenant of redemption that scripture communicates to us and we thank you for the men throughout church history who have worked through this doctrine and communicated it to us today. I ask that you would bless the rest of your Lord's day, bless the preaching of your word as your people gather to worship you, and ask all these things in the name of your son through the power of your spirit to the praise of your glorious grace. Amen.
